topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me with a jolt, a sudden, sharp memory of a summer afternoon. I knelt by the patch of disturbed soil, my hands immediately gripping the rough bark of the remaining stump. It was the maple, the one I’d painstakingly nurtured into a young sentinel when I was ten. Now, only a ragged, splintered trunk lay against the overgrown weeds. A slow, heavy feeling settled in my chest, a dull ache that refused to dissipate. I stood up, brushing the dirt from my jeans with deliberate, jerky movements. "No," I muttered to the silence, the sound rough in my throat. "That's not right." I walked to the edge of the clearing, scanning the horizon as if expecting to see the culprit materialize from the shadows. My jaw tightened, a hard line forming beneath my skin. I wouldn't accept this.

[story 2]

I found the clearing by sheer, stubborn refusal to believe what my eyes were telling me. The space where the towering oak used to stand was now an open, sun-drenched expanse, littered with the sharp, pale remnants of its fallen limbs. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I touched the ground, pressing my fingers into the soil where the roots had once anchored that giant. A hot flush crept up my neck. It felt like a betrayal, a profound wrongness that demanded immediate confrontation. "Impossible," I whispered, the word tasting like ash. I paced back and forth, my boots crunching on dry leaves. Every instinct screamed for me to run, to flee the stark reality of the removal, but I planted my feet firmly. I had to look closer, to find the flaw in the evidence, to prove this was a mistake.

[story 3]

It took me three days just to stand near the site without shaking. The silence of the empty space was deafening, amplifying the hollow feeling in my gut. I stared at the fresh, uneven cut where the base of the tree had been, the exposed wood looking brutally violated. My knuckles were white where I clenched my fists at my sides. I didn't cry; tears felt like a surrender, a weakening of the resolve I was clinging to. I circled the perimeter, my gaze sweeping over the neighboring properties, searching for any sign of disturbance, any trace of the machinery that had done this. A low growl escaped my chest, involuntary and raw. I took a deep, shuddering breath, forcing my shoulders back, refusing to let the disappointment settle into resignation. I would find out who did this.
